content.content:
1	---
2	name: gamut-director
3	description: Use when the user wants to produce a short generative video — a 10-30 second commercial, brand spot, montage, or trailer — entirely from a written brief, using only the `gamut` CLI + Fal AI backends + the web for reference. Triggers on "make a commercial", "generate a video ad", "produce a spot", "direct a video from a brief", "end-to-end generative video".
4	---
5	
6	# gamut-director — end-to-end generative video
7	
8	You are the director. Take a brief (or invent one), produce a finished MP4.
9	Every visible frame and every audible sample is AI-generated. No stock
10	footage, no hand-edited timeline, no manual asset wrangling.
11	
12	## Tools you need
13	
14	- **`gamut` CLI** — the entire pipeline. Single binary at
15	  `packages/gamut/target/debug/gamut` from the repo root, or just `gamut`
16	  if it's on PATH.
17	- **The web** — research the subject of the commercial (palette, mood,
18	  reference shots) to inform your prompts. Use WebSearch/WebFetch.
19	- **Bash** — for parallel shot generation and one final ffmpeg mux step.
20	- **`FAL_KEY`** — pre-exported in env. Don't print it. The CLI reads it.
21	
22	## The two production paths
23	
24	Two workflows, picked by whether the commercial is for a **real product**
25	(brand identity, recognizable silhouette — cars, watches, sneakers,
26	named landmarks) or a **brand-vibe** concept (fragrance, abstract
27	luxury, travel, energy drink — anything where AI hallucination is
28	acceptable).
29	
30	**Path B — reference-conditioned scene-still gen (the default for any
31	real product).** Collect 1-3 reference photos of the exact product.
32	For each scene, generate a scene-aware still that places the product
33	INTO that scene's lighting, angle, and perspective via
34	`gamut image scene-still` (Fal Seedream, ref-conditioned txt2img).
35	Then `img2vid` each scene-still. The product is generated fresh into
36	each shot's world — no cutout edges, no flat composites, no floating
37	subjects on black voids.
38	
39	```
40	web research → 1-3 high-quality reference photos (URLs)
41	            → for each scene:
42	                gamut image scene-still --refs <urls> --prompt "<scene>"
43	                gamut shot img2vid still-N.png "<motion>"
44	```
45	
46	**Path A — txt2vid only (brand-vibe only).**
47	Wan-T2V generates each shot from scratch. Cheap (~$0.10/shot), fast
48	(~30s/shot). Use for fragrance, travel, abstract concepts. Do NOT use
49	for real branded products — Wan hallucinates logos, badges, and text.
50	
51	**Legacy: bg-remove + composite (deprecated, kept for one narrow case).**
52	The old pipeline (`gamut image isolate` → `gamut shot still` →
53	`gamut image composite` → `img2vid`) still works, but it's only
54	recommended when you have a photographically-perfect reference and
55	just need to place it into a clean studio backdrop. For everything
56	else, scene-still gen avoids the cutout-seam failure mode.
57	
58	Cost per shot (Path B): $0.04 scene-still + $0.10 img2vid = $0.14.
59	About 22% more than the legacy compose pipeline ($0.115); buys away
60	cutout seams and the floating-subject look.
61	
62	**The one hard rule for Path B.**
63	
64	**Pick 1-3 high-quality reference photos of the *same* product. All
65	shots in the spot derive from those refs via scene-still gen.**
66	Different scenes come from different *scene prompts*, not from
67	different reference photos of different cars. Refs lock the product
68	identity; scene prompts vary the world around it. Use refs of the
69	same car / same watch / same sneaker (different angles of the same
70	unit are fine and helpful). Do NOT mix a 911 ref with a Cayman ref —
71	the model will blend silhouettes between shots and the identity will
72	drift.
73	
74	## The pipeline (run in order)
75	
76	```
77	brief.md (9-line) → gamut brief check
78	            │
79	            ├─ write screenplay
80	            ├─ gamut screenplay parse → screenplay.json (sanity check)
81	            ├─ gamut velocity propose → velocity.json
82	            ├─ gamut storyboard plan  → storyboard.json
83	            ├─ gamut storyboard verify → must be 0 errors
84	            ├─ gamut continuity check  → must be 0 errors
85	            ├─ gamut transitions classify → transitions.json
86	            │
87	            ├─ gamut music gen → music.wav   (paid: ~$0.06)
88	            ├─ gamut velocity validate --against music.wav  (sanity)
89	            │
90	            ├─ for each scene:
91	            │   gamut shot txt2vid → shots/shot-N.mp4 (paid: ~$0.10 each)
92	            │
93	            ├─ write scene HTML overlays
94	            ├─ build comp.json
95	            └─ gamut render + ffmpeg mux → commercial.mp4
96	```
97	
98	## Gating spend with the reviewer
99	
100	After each stage produces an artifact — storyboard JSON, hero-panels
101	set, per-shot scene-still, per-shot i2v MP4, the final muxed cut —
102	invoke the `gamut-reviewer` skill with the stage name + artifact path
103	+ the brief. It returns a structured pass/warn/fail verdict and a
104	`spend_decision` of `proceed`, `iterate`, or `abort`. Honor it: only
105	move to the next paid step on `proceed`. On `iterate`, apply the
106	named remediation (re-roll, `fix-from-verify`, tighten the prompt)
107	and re-review. On `abort` (the same fail has recurred ≥ 3 times),
108	stop and report back rather than burning more budget. The reviewer
109	only reads — it never spends — so calling it between every stage is
110	free insurance against compounding errors.
111	
112	## Step 1 — pick a concept and write the 9-line brief
113	
114	A 10-15 second commercial works best. Good fits:
115	- A consumer brand without specific logo demands (coffee, fragrance,
116	  EV concept, watch, travel)
117	- A travel destination
118	- A non-profit cause / public service spot
119	
120	Avoid:
121	- Real people with dialogue (no lip-sync yet)
122	- Specific brand logos (Wan will hallucinate them poorly)
123	- Products that need close-ups of small written details
124	- Anything requiring text legibility in the generated footage
125	
126	### The 9-line ad creative brief
127	
128	Don't write prose. Write `brief.md` in the **9-line slot-filled
129	format** popularized by veo3gen.app and now standard across the AI
130	commercial community. One slot per line, in any order:
131	
132	| Slot       | What it captures                                       |
133	|------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
134	| `PRODUCT`  | What we're selling — one noun phrase                   |
135	| `AUDIENCE` | Who the spot is for — specific demographic, not "everyone" |
136	| `INSIGHT`  | What they currently believe/feel that the brand wants to shift |
137	| `PROMISE`  | What the brand says it will deliver                    |
138	| `PROOF`    | One concrete reason to believe the promise             |
139	| `TONE`     | Single-word aesthetic register (e.g. `cinematic`, `irreverent`, `brutalist`) |
140	| `MUSIC`    | Genre + energy curve (e.g. `ambient build → driving electronic peak`) |
141	| `CALL`     | What the viewer should do — CTA in 1-5 words           |
142	| `RUNTIME`  | Target duration in seconds (integer)                   |
143	
144	Each slot maps cleanly into downstream L-Storyboard slot generation
145	(`attributes` in step 3.25) and the eventual model prompts. Forcing
146	the agent to commit to specifics on each axis — rather than writing
147	prose that hand-waves over the hard choices — is the whole point.
148	
149	Worked example (`brief.md`):
150	
151	```markdown
152	PRODUCT: Allbirds Tree Runner sneakers
153	AUDIENCE: 28-40 urban professionals who walk more than they run
154	INSIGHT: "Sustainable" usually means uncomfortable or ugly
155	PROMISE: All-day comfort that happens to be made from trees
156	PROOF: Eucalyptus-fiber upper + sugarcane sole, machine washable
157	TONE: understated
158	MUSIC: acoustic minimal → warm indie-folk swell
159	CALL: Try them barefoot
160	RUNTIME: 15
161	```
162	
163	Validate before continuing:
164	
165	```bash
166	gamut brief check brief.md
167	# OK
168	# (or: missing slots / parse errors / "fewer than 3 words" warnings)
169	```
170	
171	`gamut brief check` is pure parsing — no LLM, no network. It catches
172	missing slots, non-numeric runtimes, duplicate slots, and flags
173	suspiciously short slots (< 3 words on AUDIENCE / INSIGHT / PROMISE /
174	PROOF) or prose-shaped slots (> 20 words on TONE / CALL).
175	
176	**Long-form briefs are still acceptable as input.** When a human hands
177	you a prose brief, your job is to **distill** it into the 9-line shape
178	*before* moving to step 2. Don't pipe prose into the screenplay
179	stage — the downstream stages assume slot-filled input.
180	
181	## Step 2 — write the screenplay
182	
183	Fountain format (`.fountain`). 4-6 scenes, mostly action paragraphs.
184	Match the screenplay's pacing to the commercial: short punchy action =
185	fast cuts; long flowing description = slower scenes.
186	
187	```fountain
188	Title: <product>
189	Author: gamut-director
190	
191	EXT. SAGUARO FIELD - DAY
192	
193	A giant cactus stands sentinel against the morning sky.
194	
195	CUT TO:
196	
197	EXT. SLOT CANYON - DAY
198	
199	Light cuts through the narrow walls of red stone.
200	
201	CUT TO:
202	
203	EXT. SEDONA VISTA - SUNSET
204	
205	Cliffs glow as the sun drops behind the ridge.
206	
207	CUT TO:
208	
209	EXT. DESERT ROAD - NIGHT
210	
211	Headlights cut a path through the silence.
212	
213	FADE OUT.
214	```
215	
216	Save to `script.fountain`.
217	
218	## Step 3 — run the agent-side pipeline
219	
220	These are all free, deterministic, and reversible:
221	
222	```bash
223	gamut screenplay parse script.fountain --pretty -o screenplay.json
224	gamut velocity propose script.fountain --pretty -o velocity.json
225	gamut storyboard plan script.fountain --velocity velocity.json --pretty -o storyboard.json
226	gamut storyboard verify storyboard.json
227	gamut continuity check storyboard.json
228	gamut transitions classify script.fountain --velocity velocity.json --pretty -o transitions.json
229	```
230	
231	**Read each output.** The velocity profile's `mean_bpm` field (top
232	level of `velocity.json`) tells you the music's target tempo —
233	duration-weighted average across all anchors. `duration_secs` tells
234	you how long gamut *estimates* the screenplay would take on screen (a
235	heuristic — 2.5 wps for action, 1.2s min per dialogue beat). You
236	don't have to honor that — pass `--duration <secs>` to `music gen` to
237	hit a specific length. The storyboard's `shots[].subject` tells you
238	what each shot is about. The continuity report flags any 180° /
239	motion / scale-jump issues — if there are errors, reorder the
240	screenplay or add a transition before continuing.
241	
242	## Step 3.25 — fill structured shot attributes (L-Storyboard, encouraged)
243	
244	`Shot` now carries an optional `attributes` block — the L-Storyboard
245	schema from arXiv 2505.12237. Seven typed slots replace freeform prose
246	in the eventual model prompt:
247	
248	| Slot     | What it captures                                   |
249	|----------|----------------------------------------------------|
250	| subject  | what the shot is OF                                |
251	| action   | what's happening                                   |
252	| scene    | where it is (location + time of day + environment) |
253	| camera   | shot type + focal length + angle                   |
254	| lens     | optical character — DoF, anamorphic, fringe        |
255	| lighting | direction + quality of light                       |
256	| style    | aesthetic register, film stock, color grade        |
257	
258	When you have enough information after research, fill every slot for
259	every shot. The assembler joins them in fixed order
260	(`{subject}. {action}. {scene}. Shot: {camera}, {lens}. Lighting:
261	{lighting}. Style: {style}.`) so identical attribute sets produce
262	identical prompts across runs — important for backend caching and
263	regression tests.
264	
265	```json
266	"attributes": {
267	  "subject": "a 1968 Porsche 911 GT3 in racing yellow",
268	  "action": "idles, engine off, parked at pit lane",
269	  "scene": "on wet asphalt as the sun crests the ridge",
270	  "camera": "WS 50mm, low angle, 3/4 front",
271	  "lens": "anamorphic, shallow DoF, slight chromatic fringe",
272	  "lighting": "backlit by rising sun, mist-diffused",
273	  "style": "cinematic, A24-flavored, restrained color"
274	}
275	```
276	
277	When `attributes` is present, the per-shot prompt fragment is
278	`attributes.to_prompt()` — the legacy `Generation`-payload + shot-type
279	suffix is skipped. When `attributes` is absent (or under-specified
280	shots where you genuinely don't know the lens / lighting / style yet)
281	the freeform path keeps working unchanged.
282	
283	All seven slots are required — if you don't know one, write the literal
284	string `"unspecified"` rather than leaving it blank. Empty strings fail
285	validation; explicit "unspecified" surfaces the omission for later
286	review. Reference fixture: `packages/gamut/tests/fixtures/l-storyboard-example.json`.
287	
288	## Step 3.26 — let an LLM fill the slots (`gamut director synthesize`)
289	
290	Hand-writing seven slots per shot is mechanical work the agent
291	shouldn't have to do for every spot. `gamut director synthesize` reads
292	a brief + a storyboard skeleton (each shot already has `id` + `subject`
293	+ a `Generation` payload) and asks an LLM (Gemini 2.5 Pro by default;
294	Claude Opus 4.7 via `--model claude`) to fill every shot's seven slots
295	in one call. The orchestrator:
296	
297	- Sends the canonical creative-director system prompt + your brief +
298	  the shot list as JSON.
299	- Parses the returned `{shots:[…]}` JSON (markdown fences and leading
300	  prose are tolerated).
301	- Validates every returned shot via `ShotAttributes::validate()`.
302	- Retries ONCE with a follow-up listing any empty slots; errors after
303	  the second attempt.
304	- Merges the populated attributes into each `Shot.attributes` and
305	  writes the new storyboard.
306	
307	```bash
308	# default: Gemini 2.5 Pro via fal-ai/any-llm, ~$0.02–$0.05 / spot
309	gamut director synthesize brief.md storyboard.json -o storyboard.dir.json --pretty
310	
311	# style override applied to every shot
312	gamut director synthesize brief.md storyboard.json -o storyboard.dir.json \
313	  --style-anchor "A24-flavored, 35mm grain, dusk palette"
314	
315	# Claude Opus 4.7 fallback when Gemini's drifting on consistency
316	gamut director synthesize brief.md storyboard.json -o storyboard.dir.json --model claude
317	```
318	
319	### When to use this vs. hand-writing attributes
320	
321	| Use `director synthesize` when…             | Hand-write attributes when…                  |
322	|---------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
323	| You have a brief and 4–12 shots to fill.    | You have a specific creative vision per shot.|
324	| Lens / lighting / style should match across the spot but no shot needs a unique look. | A particular shot needs an off-pattern lens or lighting (e.g. the closer is harsh fluorescent while the rest is golden hour). |
325	| You want a reasonable starting point and will lightly tune two or three slots. | The spot is two shots — faster to write than to prompt-engineer. |
326	| The brief is rich (palette, lens family, time of day, tone references). | The brief is one sentence — the LLM will hallucinate scene specifics. |
327	
328	The LLM-written output is usually 70–80% of director-grade — same lens
329	family across shots, consistent lighting motif, reasonable camera
330	phrasing. Read the output and patch the two or three slots that drift
331	(common pattern: scene descriptions wander away from the literal
332	brief — e.g. "kitchen" becomes "design studio" in shot 2 of 4). The
333	agent's role here is *direction*, not transcription — push back when a
334	slot is wrong.
335	
336	## Step 3.5 — generate the voiceover (optional)
337	
338	If the commercial calls for narration, generate it BEFORE music so you
339	know how long the VO is (and can time the music to match).
340	
341	```bash
342	gamut dialogue tts "<your VO copy>" \
343	  --backend fal-kokoro \
344	  --voice af_nicole \
345	  --max-cost 0.05 \
346	  --out vo.wav \
347	  --pretty
348	```
349	
350	Backends:
351	- `fal-kokoro` — fast, open-weight, Fal-hosted. Default for our stack.
352	  Voice ids include `af_nicole` (female), `af_bella`, `am_adam` (male),
353	  `am_michael`. Pick one that suits the brand.
354	- `elevenlabs` — requires `ELEVENLABS_API_KEY` env var with
355	  `text_to_speech` permission on the key.
356	
357	Write VO copy that:
358	- Fits the total duration (~2.5 words/sec is a comfortable read pace,
359	  so 30s commercial ≈ 60-75 spoken words max — including pauses)
360	- Lands the brand or model name CLEARLY (preferably with a 1-second
361	  pause before for emphasis)
362	- Has a clear call-to-action or tagline at the end
363	
364	Listen to the result (`open vo.wav`) or check `audio_bytes` in the
365	JSON output to confirm it's reasonable (typically 40-60 KB/sec for
366	Kokoro WAV).
367	
368	### Word-level captions (CapCut / Hormozi / minimal)
369	
370	Kinetic captions are table-stakes for AI commercial spots in 2026 —
371	~0.25-0.4s dwell per word, single emphasis word per beat, bottom-third
372	placement. Generate them straight from the VO you just produced.
373	
374	```bash
375	# 1. Align: VO audio → per-word timestamps (JSON)
376	gamut dialogue captions \
377	  --audio vo.wav \
378	  --text "Fast cheap reliable big wins" \
379	  --backend fal-whisper-words \
380	  --max-cost 0.10 \
381	  --style hormozi \
382	  -o captions.json \
383	  --pretty
384	
385	# 2. Render the HTML overlay
386	gamut captions overlay \
387	  --in captions.json \
388	  --style hormozi \
389	  --width 1080 --height 1920 \
390	  -o caption.html
391	```
392	
393	Two backends:
394	- `fal-whisper-words` — Fal-hosted Whisper with `chunk_level: "word"`.
395	  Native per-word `[start_ms, end_ms]` timestamps. ~$0.001 per 10s of
396	  audio. Local files are uploaded to fal-storage automatically.
397	- `synthetic` — distributes the VO duration evenly across the words of
398	  `--text`. Requires `--duration-ms`. No API call, no cost, no
399	  intonation awareness. Use as a fallback when `FAL_KEY` is unset.
400	
401	Three style presets:
402	- **`hormozi`** — single word at a time, large bottom-center, yellow
403	  highlight on the emphasis word per beat (longest word in each
404	  4-word window; ALL-CAPS words outrank longer mixed-case neighbours).
405	  Dwell ~0.25-0.4s. Best for high-energy direct-response spots.
406	- **`capcut`** — sliding groups of 2-3 words, soft fade from below,
407	  dwell ~0.5-0.8s per group. Best for "explainer" / lifestyle pacing.
408	- **`minimal`** — full sentence at once, classic broadcast lower-third
409	  with a single fade in/out. Best for editorial spots and luxury brands
410	  where word-by-word kinetics feel cheap.
411	
412	Drop the emitted `caption.html` into your scene overlay flow as a sibling
413	HTML scene with the same duration as the VO. The CSS uses `@keyframes`
414	only — no JS — so it renders correctly under Blitz / RVST / headless
415	browsers without a runtime.
416	
417	Emphasis detection is a v0 heuristic (longest word, ALL-CAPS preferred).
418	Future work: ML-driven prosody emphasis (TBD follow-up issue).
419	
420	## Step 4 — generate the music
421	
422	```bash
423	gamut music gen \
424	  --velocity velocity.json \
425	  --style "<2-3 line style description>" \
426	  --duration <total_secs> \
427	  --max-cost 0.10 \
428	  --out music.mp3 \
429	  --pretty
430	```
431	
432	### Backend — commercial safety matters
433	
434	The default `--backend elevenlabs` (ElevenLabs Music) is the **only
435	commercially-safe** option. ElevenLabs licenses its training catalog
436	through Merlin + Kobalt — the two largest music-industry rights
437	aggregators — so output is licensed for commercial use by construction.
438	
439	- `--backend elevenlabs` *(default)* — Merlin + Kobalt-licensed.
440	  Requires `ELEVENLABS_API_KEY` with the `music_generation` permission
441	  (TTS-only keys 401 on this endpoint). Output is MP3. Min duration 3s,
442	  max 600s.
443	- `--backend udio` — Udio partnership tier, alternative for orchestral
444	  hero cues. Live calls are not yet wired (dry-run works; planned in
445	  wb-23j2).
446	- `--backend fal-musicgen` — **DEPRECATED**. MusicGen has pending
447	  training-data litigation; output is **not** commercial-safe. Kept
448	  for experimentation only; the CLI prints a deprecation warning on
449	  every invocation.
450	
451	Style prompt guidance:
452	- Match the commercial's mood (luxury → "cinematic strings, slow build,
453	  warm reverb"; energy → "driving percussion, kick drum, electronic")
454	- Include "no vocals" if the music shouldn't compete with VO
455	- The CLI auto-appends the velocity arc to your prompt, so don't repeat
456	  BPM markers — just describe the *style*
457	
458	Validate:
459	```bash
460	gamut velocity validate velocity.json --against music.wav --tolerance 20 --pretty
461	```
462	
463	The validator looks for percussive onsets. Soft ambient music produces
464	0 detected onsets; that's not a failure, just means the validator
465	can't grade it. If you want strong validation, prompt for a percussive
466	style.
467	
468	### Snap shot boundaries to onsets
469	
470	`velocity validate` now also writes a sibling `music.cuts.edl` (FCP7 /
471	Resolve compatible) when any onsets were detected, and the JSON report
472	carries a `detected_onsets_ms` field with the full onset list. Use
473	those onsets as snap targets for shot boundaries — per Curious Refuge,
474	"snap title cards to the loudest onset within ±1 frame". To regenerate
475	just the EDL without re-validating:
476	
477	```bash
478	gamut velocity onsets-to-edl --music music.wav --fps 30 -o cuts.edl
479	```
480	
481	This composes with frame-chaining (wb-fr2g/G1, already shipped):
482	onset-snapped shot boundaries + first-last-frame chaining together
483	give beat-aligned, visually-continuous cuts. Suppress the sibling EDL
484	with `--no-emit-edl` if you only want the JSON report.
485	
486	## Step 5 — generate the shots
487	
488	### Path A: txt2vid (brand-vibe)
489	
490	One `gamut shot txt2vid` call per scene. **Run them in parallel**
491	(separate Bash calls with `run_in_background: true`) since each takes
492	30-90 seconds.
493	
494	```bash
495	gamut shot txt2vid \
496	  "<rich, specific prompt>" \
497	  --duration 5 \
498	  --max-cost 0.20 \
499	  --out shots/shot-N-<name>.mp4 \
500	  --pretty
501	```
502	
503	Prompt construction (in this order, comma-separated):
504	1. Subject (`a giant saguaro cactus`)
505	2. Action (`stands sentinel`)
506	3. Setting (`Arizona desert at golden hour`)
507	4. Composition (`wide low-angle shot`)
508	5. Atmosphere (`dramatic clouds, lens flare`)
509	6. Tech (`cinematic, photorealistic, shallow depth of field`)
510	
511	Each shot is 5 seconds at 16fps native (Wan-T2V default). The renderer
512	handles fps mismatch automatically and holds the last frame if a scene
513	overruns the clip (so request scene durations ≤ 5s, or generate longer
514	shots upstream).
515	
516	#### Negative prompts — the standard set is automatic
517	
518	Every `gamut shot txt2vid` / `img2vid` call automatically appends a
519	canonical negative prompt — `"no text overlay, no watermark, no
520	distortion, no extra limbs, no extra fingers, low quality, blurry"` —
521	documented to cut unusable outputs by ~30% per the Artlist 2026 guide.
522	You never have to type the standard negatives. Only use `--negative`
523	when the shot needs *additional* don'ts on top of the default (e.g.
524	`--negative "morphing, jittery motion"` for hand-animation shots). The
525	adapter merges your addition with the default. Pass
526	`--no-default-negatives` only for adversarial experiments.
527	
528	### Path B: reference-conditioned scene-still gen (2-step pipeline per shot)
529	
530	**Step B.1 — Collect 1-3 reference photos of the exact product.**
531	Use `WebSearch` to find direct image URLs (not page URLs) — Wikipedia
532	and manufacturer press kits are good sources. Prefer photos with:
533	
534	- Solo subject (no bystanders, no other vehicles in frame)
535	- Neutral, even-light backgrounds (parking lot, studio, plain road)
536	- No baked-in watermarks (check the corners)
537	
538	Why 1-3 and not always 1: a side profile + a front 3/4 + a rear angle
539	of the *same unit* lets the scene-still model understand the
540	silhouette from multiple sides. All refs must be the same product —
541	same model, same color, same trim, same year.
542	
543	> HTTPS URLs only for now. `wb-m9qe` added local-path support to the
544	> isolate path, but the scene-still CLI hasn't been updated to use it
545	> yet. If you find that's been done, the `--refs` flag should accept
546	> local paths transparently; otherwise file a follow-up.
547	
548	**Step B.2 — For each scene, generate a scene-aware still.**
549	
550	```bash
551	gamut image scene-still \
552	  --refs "https://.../ref-1.jpg,https://.../ref-2.jpg" \
553	  --prompt "the car at golden hour on a coastal cliff highway, wide low-angle, dramatic side-light, no other vehicles" \
554	  --image-size landscape_16_9 \
555	  --max-cost 0.05 \
556	  --out shots/still-N.png \
557	  --pretty
558	```
559	
560	Wraps Fal Seedream (reference-conditioned txt2img). The refs lock the
561	product identity; the prompt describes the scene's lighting, angle,
562	camera, and atmosphere. Be specific:
563	
564	- ✓ `"the car at golden hour on a coastal cliff highway, wide
565	   low-angle, dramatic side-light, no other vehicles"`
566	- ✓ `"the watch resting on a polished black stone slab, top-down
567	   macro, single soft key light from upper-left, deep shadows"`
568	- ✗ `"a car driving fast"` (no scene detail; the model has nothing
569	   to hang the product on)
570	
571	Scene prompts vary; the `--refs` list stays the same across every
572	shot in the spot. That's what gives the product identity continuity.
573	
574	**Hero-frame pre-gen (Curious Refuge "lock the look").** Before running
575	per-shot scene-still calls, generate a single 4-panel composite covering
576	the spot's 4 key moments — one Seedream call returns a 1×4 strip (or
577	2×2 grid) where the model is forced to hold consistent palette,
578	lighting direction, and color temperature across all four panels
579	simultaneously. Crop the composite into per-shot reference stills,
580	then condition every per-shot scene-still on the matching panel.
581	
582	```bash
583	gamut storyboard hero-panels storyboard.json \
584	  -o shots/hero/ \
585	  --refs "https://.../ref-1.jpg" \
586	  --layout 2x2 \
587	  --max-cost 0.10 --pretty
588	# writes shots/hero/composite.png + panel-{1..4}.png + panels.json
589	
590	# Then for each downstream scene-still, pass the matching panel:
591	gamut image scene-still \
592	  --hero-panel-ref "https://.../panel-2.png" \
593	  --refs "https://.../ref-1.jpg" \
594	  --prompt "..." --max-cost 0.05 --out shots/still-2.png
595	```
596	
597	Cost: $0.04 hero-panels + 4 × $0.04 scene-still = $0.20 total vs $0.16
598	without — one extra Seedream call for substantially better
599	shot-to-shot palette + lighting coherence across the spot. Seedream
600	sometimes returns a 2×2 layout even when you ask for 1×4; pass
601	`--layout 2x2` when the storyboard is portrait/square or when you
602	want denser tiles. The `panels.json` manifest maps shot ids → panel
603	paths so the per-shot calls always pick the right one.
604	
605	**Two-tier still gen.** The first 1-2 shots (the ones that define the
606	spot's "look") should run through the premium tier — they lock the
607	palette, lighting, and product placement that every later shot
608	conditions on. Bulk shots run on the cheap tier conditioned on the
609	hero stills.
610	
611	- **Hero shots:** `--backend nano-banana-pro` (~$0.24/img, up to **14**
612	  refs, **4K** output, 94-96% in-image text accuracy). Pass your raw
613	  product refs.
614	- **Bulk shots:** default `--backend seedream` (~$0.04/img, up to 10
615	  refs). Pass the hero stills as `--refs` so palette + lighting carry
616	  across the spot.
617	
618	```bash
619	# Hero shot — locks the spot's look
620	gamut image scene-still --backend nano-banana-pro \
621	  --refs "https://.../ref-1.jpg,https://.../ref-2.jpg" \
622	  --prompt "..." --max-cost 0.30 --out shots/hero-1.png
623	
624	# Bulk shot — conditioned on the hero still (upload hero to a public URL
625	# first; local-path refs ship with wb-m9qe)
626	gamut image scene-still \
627	  --refs "https://.../hero-1.png,https://.../ref-1.jpg" \
628	  --prompt "..." --max-cost 0.05 --out shots/still-3.png
629	```
630	
631	**Step B.3 — img2vid each scene-still.**
632	
633	```bash
634	gamut shot img2vid shots/still-N.png \
635	  "<motion prompt>" \
636	  --duration 5 --max-cost 0.15 \
637	  --out shots/shot-N.mp4 \
638	  --pretty
639	```
640	
641	Because the scene-still already contains a full world (lighting,
642	horizon, ground plane, subject in correct perspective), i2v produces
643	meaningful camera motion rather than tiny nudges around a floating
644	cutout.
645	
646	Motion prompts (keep SHORT — 1 sentence max):
647	- `slow push in, cinematic parallax`
648	- `dolly left to right`
649	- `slight reveal, the subject emerges from shadow`
650	- `subtle handheld float, atmospheric`
651	
652	### Validating each shot
653	
654	Extract a frame at t=2.5s of each shot and confirm:
655	1. The subject is recognizable as the named product (and matches the
656	   refs — same color, same trim, same silhouette)
657	2. The subject sits naturally in the scene (correct ground contact,
658	   plausible scale, lighting matches the scene prompt)
659	3. No bystanders / watermarks / other vehicles hallucinated in
660	
661	If a shot fails: re-roll `scene-still` with a sharper scene prompt or
662	swap in a better ref. The expensive step (i2v at $0.10) only re-runs
663	on actually-different inputs, so iterate on the still first.
664	
665	Budget for Path B per shot: ~$0.14
666	(`scene-still $0.04` + `img2vid $0.10`).
667	
668	### Variant generation — roll N, pick the winner
669	
670	Every still / clip gen verb accepts `--variants N` (1-8, default 1).
671	With `N > 1` the verb runs N gens in parallel with `seed = base + i`
672	and emits a JSON manifest pointing at all N cached results plus the
673	winner. Each variant lands in its own cache slot keyed on
674	`request_hash + seed`, so re-runs are free.
675	
676	```bash
677	gamut image scene-still \
678	  --refs https://… --prompt "…hero shot…" \
679	  --variants 3 --select max-vlm \
680	  --max-cost 0.05 --max-variants-cost 0.15 \
681	  --pretty
682	```
683	
684	When to roll variants:
685	
686	- `--variants 3` for **hero shots** and **identity-critical SKUs**
687	  (the product on a transparent background, the closing logo plate,
688	  any frame the eye lingers on). One-shot picks too often hallucinate
689	  badge text or warp silhouette.
690	- `--variants 1` (default) for **filler shots**, **draft tier**, and
691	  **anything cost-bound**. Re-rolling 3× a $0.10 i2v clip is $0.30 per
692	  shot — not free.
693	
694	`--select` policies:
695	
696	| Policy | When to use |
697	|---|---|
698	| `max-vlm` (default) | Production — VLM-grades each variant against the brief's negative criteria; highest pass-rate wins. Tie-break by first-by-seed. Stills only — clips fall back to `first`. |
699	| `pairwise-tournament` | Hero shots, identity-critical SKUs — VISTA-style bracket (multi-dim VLM critique). See below. |
700	| `first` | Debug / mock. Variant 0 wins. |
701	| `user` | Emit all N + manifest; you (or the agent) pick interactively. `winner` is `null`. |
702	| `cheapest` | Debug only — whichever cached fastest. |
703	
704	Cost gating:
705	
706	- `--max-cost` continues to apply per call.
707	- `--max-variants-cost <USD>` is the **aggregate ceiling** across all N
708	  variants. The pre-call line prints `variants=3 estimated cost =
709	  $0.12 (3 × $0.04)` so the agent can read it before spending. For
710	  `pairwise-tournament` the pre-call line splits gen + judging:
711	  `variants=4 pairwise gen=$0.1600 + judging=$0.0300 = $0.1900`.
712	- If one variant errors out, the rest still produce a winner — the
713	  errored variant appears in the manifest with an `error` field.
714	
715	#### VISTA tournament (`--select pairwise-tournament`)
716	
717	`max-vlm` reduces every variant to a single integer (`pass_count`).
718	That works for catching obvious failures (extra limbs, baked text)
719	but loses signal whenever two variants both pass every criterion —
720	the tie-break falls to seed order, which is arbitrary.
721	
722	`pairwise-tournament` (per [arXiv 2510.15831](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.15831))
723	replaces the single-dim score with a **single-elimination bracket**
724	of pair-wise VLM critiques. Each pair, the VLM grades A vs B across
725	four dimensions and returns one of `{A, B, tie}` per dimension:
726	
727	1. **Subject fidelity** — identity preserved, right product, right brand.
728	2. **Composition** — rule-of-thirds, negative space, no centered-and-flat.
729	3. **Lighting + color** — coherent with the prompt's atmosphere.
730	4. **Production polish** — sharpness, no artifacts, no anatomy errors.
731	
732	The pair winner is A if A wins ≥ 3 dimensions OR `(2 wins + 2 ties)`,
733	symmetrically for B, otherwise lower-seed wins (`seed_tiebreak: true`
734	in the manifest). Bracket cost grows as `N - 1` pairs:
735	
736	| N variants | Pair calls | Judging cost |
737	|---|---|---|
738	| 2 | 1 | ~$0.01 |
739	| 4 | 3 | ~$0.03 |
740	| 8 | 7 | ~$0.07 |
741	
742	When to use which:
743	
744	- `pairwise-tournament` — **hero shots**, **identity-critical SKUs**,
745	  the closing logo plate, any frame the eye lingers on. The per-pair
746	  rationale ends up in the manifest, so the selection is auditable
747	  (every round records the dim-level verdicts + a one-sentence
748	  explanation).
749	- `max-vlm` — everything else. Faster (1 VLM call vs N - 1 sequential
750	  pair calls), cheaper, and good enough for filler shots and draft
751	  tier.
752	
753	The manifest grows a `bracket` array with one entry per match:
754	
755	```json
756	{
757	  "round": "semi-1",
758	  "a_seed": 0, "b_seed": 1,
759	  "winner": 0,
760	  "judgments": {
761	    "subject_fidelity": "tie",
762	    "composition": "A",
763	    "lighting_color": "A",
764	    "production": "tie",
765	    "rationale": "A's framing exploits negative space; lighting on A is more cinematic."
766	  }
767	}
768	```
769	
770	The same `--criteria` flag feeds into pairwise — the criteria get
771	joined as the `brief_excerpt` the VLM uses to anchor its judging.
772	
773	## Step 6 — write the scene HTML overlays (be creative)
774	
775	One `scenes/<id>.html` per scene composites *over* the generated video.
776	The renderer's HTML engine is **Blitz** — a real CSS engine with Stylo
777	(Servo's parallel CSS engine) and Parley for text shaping. It supports
778	the full web platform: CSS animations (`@keyframes`, transitions),
779	`transform`, `clip-path`, blend modes, gradients (linear/radial/conic),
780	SVG, Web fonts via `@font-face`, CSS variables, masking, filters,
781	flexbox/grid. Use any of it.
782	
783	### Only two structural rules
784	
785	1. **`html` and `body` must have `background: transparent`** so the
786	   generated video shows through where your HTML doesn't paint.
787	2. **No `<img>` or `<video>` tags** — those layers come from
788	   `comp.json` (`video_bg` for the moving image, `audio_cues` for
789	   sound).
790	
791	Everything else is yours. Write the HTML the way you would for a
792	hand-crafted website.
793	
794	### Creative ambition — innovate on every spot
795	
796	Every commercial should look like it was art-directed by a different
797	designer. **Do not** ship the same Inter-88px-bottom-left lockup on
798	every spot — that's the recognizable "AI-default" pattern that flattens
799	the work. Make text part of the *concept*, not chrome on top of the
800	video.
801	
802	### Principles, not templates
803	
804	Read these once, then design freely. They aren't a checklist you walk
805	through scene by scene — they're the constraints that decide whether a
806	move you made works. Most of them have a tool you can call to verify
807	the decision after the fact.
808	
809	**Edge insets.** Title-safe is the center 80% of the frame, roughly a
810	10% inset off each edge. Action-safe is the center 90%, a 5% inset.
811	Type that crosses title-safe is a deliberate choice — the kind a
812	brutalist or display-driven treatment makes on purpose. Type that
813	crosses action-safe is a mistake on most platforms; phones, broadcast
814	crops, and inset UI will eat it. The math lives in
815	`gamut::aspect::safe_areas(w, h)` and returns the right rectangles per
816	aspect ratio, so you don't hard-code pixel values that go wrong the
817	moment the spot reframes from 16:9 to 9:16.
818	
819	**Lower-thirds is a convention, not the convention.** It's the right
820	move when the spot wants editorial calm — luxury, documentary, news,
821	anything where the image is the subject and type is the caption. It's
822	the wrong move when the spot wants energy or kinetic motion — sport,
823	energy drink, performance tech. In those, lower-thirds reads as
824	broadcast-news pastiche and kills the pulse. Don't reach for it by
825	default just because every YouTube tutorial does.
826	
827	**Read the shot first.** A scene-still is a real artifact, not a
828	sketch. Generate it, then run `gamut image negative-space <png>` to see
829	where the eye-friendly zones actually are. The tool returns a ranked
830	list of grid cells with a suggested text color and scrim opacity for
831	each. Position type in the top-ranked zone unless you have a reason to
832	fight the shot. Designing typography without looking at the underlying
833	frame is how you get text laid over a face.
834	
835	**Contrast minimums.** WCAG AA is 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for
836	18pt+ or 14pt bold. After you pick a region, call `gamut image contrast
837	<png> --region X,Y,W,H --text-color #...` — if the ratio comes back
838	below threshold, the tool suggests a scrim color and opacity that would
839	lift it above. Below AA is a deliberate choice (a near-invisible
840	luxury whisper, a brutalist clash), not an accident. If you're shipping
841	below AA, you should know why.
842	
843	**Reading rate.** A comfortable on-screen read is about 2.5 characters
844	per second, which means a one-second beat fits roughly 25 characters
845	before viewers fall behind. Kinetic typography that flashes per word
846	lives in the 0.25–0.4-second range; faster than 0.15s reads as a
847	flicker, not a word. Set dwell times against the string length, not
848	against the cut.
849	
850	**Motion-vs-text legibility tradeoff.** Fast camera motion plus tight
851	type equals unreadable. You have two ways out: hold the camera and let
852	the type breathe, or use heavier weights, shorter strings, and longer
853	dwells. As a rough rule, if the underlying shot has more than ~50% of
854	frame motion per second, cap the visible text at three or four words at
855	a time. Don't try to letter-space-track over a whip pan.
856	
857	**Check what's already in the shot.** Generated stills sometimes have
858	baked-in text — license plates, storefront signage, watermarks,
859	synthetic logos. Run `gamut image ocr <png>` to detect existing text
860	and avoid stacking overlays on top of it. (The OCR command is currently
861	stubbed pending the Fal `got-ocr` queue adapter; when that lands, this
862	becomes a hard check rather than a hint.)
863	
864	**Vary across scenes.** Within a single spot, no two adjacent scenes
865	should share the same typographic treatment. Same typeface is fine —
866	that's the through-line. Same size *and* position *and* motion is the
867	"AI-default" tell. Variation across cuts isn't decoration; it's the
868	design.
869	
870	The directions in the next section are starting points, not templates —
871	freely combine, invert, ignore.
872	
873	Some directions to consider (mix and match across scenes within a single
874	spot — different treatments for different moments):
875	
876	- **Brutalist** — Helvetica Black, all-caps, no kerning, text covers
877	  60-80% of the frame. Tight to the edges. Anti-pretty.
878	- **Editorial** — Didone serif (Bodoni, Playfair), generous
879	  letterspacing, two-line typography moments centered low.
880	- **Kinetic** — single word per beat, animated in via `@keyframes`,
881	  enter+exit fast (200ms cubic-bezier). Text IS the shot.
882	- **Mono / tech** — JetBrains Mono or IBM Plex Mono, small (16-22px),
883	  corner-tagged with running timestamps or "loc/spec/version" labels.
884	- **Luxury** — thin sans (Inter 200), letter-spacing wide (`0.3em`),
885	  dead-center, sub-22px, almost-invisible whisper.
886	- **Display-driven** — a single massive number (year, model, spec)
887	  fills the frame, the video bleeds through the negative space via
888	  blend modes.
889	- **Typographic mask** — `clip-path: url(#mask)` or
890	  `mix-blend-mode: difference` so the type carves into the video.
891	- **No type at all** — some scenes are pure visual. Don't feel obligated
892	  to caption every cut.
893	
894	These are **inspiration**, not templates. Compose freely. Use CSS
895	animations for motion (`@keyframes`, `transition`). Vary the typeface,
896	weight, size, position, motion, and blend mode across scenes within
897	the same spot.
898	
899	### Web fonts
900	
901	Load via `@font-face` against a CDN or a local file. Google Fonts CSS
902	works (`@import url(...)`). Don't trust a single font to carry a whole
903	spot — load 2-3 if you want type-system contrast between scenes.
904	
905	### When you're stuck
906	
907	Look at how real ad agencies treat type. Apple's product pages, Nike's
908	launch films, Aesop's homepage, Bottega Veneta's seasonal campaigns,
909	Porsche's own marketing. Don't imitate — *steal the structural moves*
910	(asymmetric layout, mid-shot title cards, kinetic reveals, typographic
911	silence).
912	
913	## Step 7 — build `comp.json`
914	
915	Schema:
916	
917	```json
918	{
919	  "width": 1280,
920	  "height": 720,
921	  "fps": 30,
922	  "duration_frames": 360,
923	  "scenes": [
924	    {
925	      "html_path": "scenes/01-title.html",
926	      "video_bg": "shots/shot-1-saguaro.mp4",
927	      "start_frame": 0,
928	      "duration_frames": 90,
929	      "motion": [
930	        { "selector": "#title", "props": [["y", 50.0], ["opacity", 0.0]], "duration_secs": 0.8, "easing": "EaseOutQuart", "start_at_secs": 0.2, "kind": "from" }
931	      ]
932	    }
933	  ],
934	  "audio_cues": [
935	    {
936	      "id": "music",
937	      "asset_path": "music.wav",
938	      "start_frame": 0,
939	      "duration_frames": 360,
940	      "volume": 0.7,
941	      "pan": 0.0,
942	      "fade_in_frames": 6,
943	      "fade_out_frames": 24,
944	      "duck_targets": [],
945	      "duck_db": 0.0
946	    }
947	  ]
948	}
949	```
950	
951	Required fields per scene: `html_path`, `start_frame`, `duration_frames`.
952	Optional: `video_bg`, `motion`, `transition_in`.
953	
954	Frame math: at 30fps, `duration_frames = 30 * secs`. For 12 seconds,
955	`duration_frames` is 360 total. Each scene's `duration_frames` should
956	sum to the total. Don't leave gaps.
957	
958	Motion easings: `Linear`, `EaseInQuart`, `EaseOutQuart`, `EaseInOutQuart`,
959	`EaseOutBack`, etc. (Animato's catalog.)
960	
961	Motion property names: `x`, `y`, `opacity`, `scale`, `rotation`.
962	
963	Motion `kind`: `from` (animate from these values *to* the element's
964	current style) or `to` (animate from current *to* these values).
965	
966	Reference: `packages/gamut/examples/arizona/comp.json` is a working
967	4-scene composition with motion + transitions.

969	## Step 8 — render and mux
970	
971	```bash
972	gamut render comp.json -o final.mp4
973	ffmpeg -y -i final.mp4 -i final.wav \
974	  -c:v copy -c:a aac -b:a 192k -shortest \
975	  commercial.mp4
976	```
977	
978	The render emits video-only MP4 plus a sidecar WAV. ffmpeg muxes them
979	into one file with embedded audio. **The muxed file is what you ship.**
